# How to use data analytics in Behavior-Based Safety programs?

![How can you analyze audit trail data for insights into EHS performance?](https://sbnsoftware.com/wp-content/uploads/PastedGraphic-1-930x279.png)

\*\*Introduction\*\*

In today’s fast-paced and increasingly complex workplace environments, maintaining safety is paramount. Behavior-Based Safety (BBS) programs have emerged as an essential strategy to enhance workplace safety by focusing on the behaviors that contribute to accidents and injuries. Unlike traditional safety approaches that often emphasize compliance with rules or procedures, BBS programs advocate for a proactive stance on human behavior, identifying and modifying unsafe practices before they result in harm. However, the effectiveness of these programs can be significantly amplified through the integration of data analytics, transforming how organizations collect, analyze, and utilize behavioral data.

Data analytics provide an invaluable toolkit that enables organizations to identify trends, predict incidents, and refine their safety interventions based on empirical evidence. By harnessing quantitative data derived from employee observations, incident reports, and environmental factors, organizations can create a nuanced understanding of the underlying patterns that influence safety-related behaviors. Moreover, data analytics foster a culture of continuous improvement by allowing teams to make data-driven decisions, rather than relying solely on intuition or anecdotal evidence. This melding of behavioral science and data analysis not only enhances the effectiveness of BBS initiatives but also fosters accountability and engagement among employees, who can see firsthand how their behavior impacts overall safety performance.

In this article, we will explore the essential steps to effectively use data analytics within Behavior-Based Safety programs. From the initial stages of data collection and the types of data that are most beneficial, to the analysis techniques that reveal meaningful patterns, we will outline the comprehensive approach toward integrating analytics into BBS. Furthermore, we will examine real-world case studies that illustrate successful applications of data analytics in BBS programs, demonstrating how organizations have leveraged this innovative approach to create safer, healthier workplaces. By equipping safety professionals with the knowledge and tools to implement data analytics, we can foster a more proactive safety culture that not only reduces incidents but also enhances employee well-being.

## Data Collection Methods in Behavior-Based Safety

Data collection methods in Behavior-Based Safety (BBS) programs are essential for understanding and improving workplace safety and employee behavior. BBS focuses on identifying and modifying unsafe behaviors through observation and feedback. To effectively implement this approach, organizations must develop robust data collection methods that capture the necessary information to not only assess current safety practices but also to inform ongoing improvements.

There are several primary data collection methods utilized in BBS programs. Direct observation is one of the most common techniques, where trained observers watch and record employee behaviors in real-time. This method allows organizations to gather firsthand insight into safety practices and identify both safe and unsafe behaviors. Observations can be structured, where specific criteria are assessed, or unstructured, allowing observers to record general behaviors and interactions. Coupled with observational data, surveys and questionnaires can be distributed to employees to gauge their perception of safety practices and to capture self-reported behaviors that might not be visible to an observer.

Another method of data collection is incident reporting, where employees report near misses, accidents, or unsafe conditions. These reports can be invaluable in identifying patterns of behavior leading to incidents. Furthermore, technology plays a significant role in modern data collection. Organizations can leverage mobile applications and software tools to streamline the reporting process, gather data more efficiently, and enhance participation rates among employees. Additionally, integrating wearable technology can provide insights into employee behaviors and environmental conditions, allowing for a more nuanced understanding of safety dynamics on the job site.

Analyzing the data collected through these methods is crucial for the success of BBS programs. Data analysis can uncover trends, highlight areas of concern, and determine the effectiveness of safety interventions. By interpreting this data, organizations can create tailored training and development programs aimed at correcting unsafe behaviors, reinforcing safe practices, and fostering a culture of safety. Moreover, engaging employees in the data collection process can cultivate ownership of safety behaviors, making them more likely to participate actively in changing unsafe practices.

In summary, employing effective data collection methods in Behavior-Based Safety programs is foundational for fostering a culture of safety within an organization. Through observation, reporting, and technology integration, organizations can acquire essential data that informs safety strategies, enhances employee involvement, and ultimately leads to a safer workplace environment.

## Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) for Safety Analytics

Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) are essential metrics used to evaluate the effectiveness of Behavior-Based Safety (BBS) programs. These indicators provide organizations with measurable values that can be closely monitored to assess safety performance, identifying areas for improvement and facilitating informed decision-making. By establishing clear and relevant KPIs, companies can focus their efforts on specific safety goals, ensuring that their BBS initiatives align with overall workplace safety objectives.

In the context of Behavior-Based Safety programs, KPIs might include metrics such as the frequency of safety observations, participation rates in safety training sessions, the number of reported near misses, and the rate of safety incidents per employee. Additionally, behaviors that contribute to safety, such as the adherence to safety protocols and the successful execution of safety measures, can also be included as KPIs. By tracking these indicators, organizations can gauge the success of their safety initiatives and measure progress toward reducing workplace incidents.

To effectively utilize KPIs in BBS programs, organizations should start by defining specific, measurable, achievable, relevant, and time-bound (SMART) goals. This approach allows companies to set clear expectations and benchmarks for safety performance. For example, a company might aim to reduce the incident rate by 20% over the next year by implementing targeted training and observation programs. Regularly reviewing and analyzing KPI data helps in identifying trends and patterns, guiding the necessary adjustments to improve safety outcomes and overall program effectiveness.

Moreover, the integration of real-time data analytics into BBS programs allows organizations to respond swiftly to emerging safety issues. By continuously monitoring KPIs, safety teams can identify shifts in behavior before they translate into incidents. This proactive approach fosters a culture of safety within the organization, encouraging workers to engage in safe practices and supporting a shared commitment to safety across all levels. In summary, KPIs are vital in driving the success of Behavior-Based Safety programs, enabling organizations to systematically track improvements, celebrate successes, and address challenges in workplace safety.

## Data Analysis Techniques for Behavior Patterns

In the realm of Behavior-Based Safety (BBS), data analysis techniques play a crucial role in identifying and understanding behavior patterns that contribute to workplace safety incidents or near misses. These techniques allow organizations to sift through the vast amounts of data collected from safety observations, incident reports, and employee feedback to uncover trends and areas for improvement. The primary goal is to transform raw data into actionable insights that can drive effective safety interventions.

One essential data analysis technique is root cause analysis, which goes beyond simply identifying that an incident occurred. Instead, it digs into the underlying behaviors and environmental factors that led to the incident. By applying methodologies such as the “Five Whys” or fishbone diagrams, organizations can systematically trace back from the incident to its root causes, allowing them to address not just the symptoms but the actual behaviors that need to change.

Another important technique involves the use of statistical analysis methods to detect patterns and correlations in safety data. This can include descriptive statistics that summarize data points, inferential statistics that help infer conclusions from sample data, and even predictive analytics that use historical data to predict future incidents. For example, by employing regression analysis, organizations can assess how various factors—such as training frequency or employee engagement levels—affect safety behavior and outcomes. Predictive models can enable organizations to focus their resources where they are likely to make the most impact, thereby preventing incidents before they happen.

Visualization tools also significantly enhance data analysis in BBS programs. Graphs, dashboards, and heat maps can present complex data in a more understandable format, making it easier for safety professionals and management to spot trends at a glance. For instance, a heat map showing high incident rates in specific departments can indicate where targeted behavior modification strategies might be necessary.

Moreover, combining multiple data sources, such as employee surveys, audit results, and incident reports, can provide a comprehensive view of the safety culture within an organization. Using multi-dimensional data analysis techniques enables safety professionals to segment data by various demographics, such as job role, seniority, or location, thereby revealing specific behavior patterns that may affect different groups within the workforce.

In summary, leveraging robust data analysis techniques in Behavior-Based Safety programs allows organizations to not only identify behavior patterns leading to safety incidents but also to implement strategic interventions. By understanding the behaviors that contribute to safety performance, and using statistical and visualization tools to clarify insights, organizations can create a proactive safety culture that ultimately reduces accidents and enhances overall workplace safety.

## Creating Actionable Insights from Safety Data

Creating actionable insights from safety data is a crucial step in enhancing Behavior-Based Safety (BBS) programs. The process typically begins with comprehensive data collection methods that gather information on observed behaviors, incident reports, and safety audits. Once the data is collected, it must be analyzed to identify trends, patterns, and areas for improvement. The goal is to transform raw data into insights that can lead to meaningful changes in safety practices and outcomes.

Data analytics in this context often involves utilizing various software tools and statistical methods to interpret the data effectively. By employing these analytical techniques, organizations can pinpoint specific behaviors that may lead to accidents or unsafe conditions. For instance, analyzing the frequency of safety violations can reveal which actions are most commonly disregarded, helping safety personnel to focus training and intervention strategies on these key areas. Additionally, it can uncover correlations between certain behaviors and incident occurrences, thus allowing organizations to understand the root causes of safety breaches.

To ensure these insights lead to action, organizations must involve all stakeholders in the discussion of safety data. This includes management, safety professionals, and employees who can provide context to the numbers. Workshops or meetings can facilitate dialogue around the insights derived from the data, fostering a sense of ownership among team members. Furthermore, developing clear action plans based on these insights is essential. It may involve retraining employees, revising safety protocols, or enhancing communication strategies related to safety practices.

The ultimate aim of creating actionable insights from safety data is to drive improvements in workplace safety culture and performance. Organizations must commit to iterative processes, where feedback from the implementation of these action plans is used to refine further data collection and analysis activities. By continuously cycling through these stages, companies can build a robust BBS program that not only responds to past incidents but also proactively addresses potential risks, leading to a safer work environment.

![Simple But Needed blog banner image](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns=%22http://www.w3.org/2000/svg%22%20viewBox=%220%200%20210%20140%22%3E%3C/svg%3E)

## Continuous Improvement and Feedback Loops in Safety Programs

Continuous improvement and feedback loops are critical components of effective Behavior-Based Safety (BBS) programs. These elements ensure that safety initiatives are not static; instead, they evolve based on real-time data and insights derived from both the observation of behaviors and outcomes. The concept of continuous improvement hinges on the idea that organizations can consistently foster enhancements in safety performance through iterative processes.

In a BBS program, continuous improvement involves regular monitoring and assessment of safety practices at various levels within the organization. By utilizing data analytics, organizations can identify trends, measure the effectiveness of existing safety interventions, and uncover areas that require enhancement. This process often utilizes key performance indicators (KPIs) to evaluate success and establish benchmarks. For instance, if an organization identifies a high frequency of near misses in a specific area, data analytics can enable the team to delve deeper into the contributing factors and design targeted interventions aimed at reducing those incidents.

Feedback loops play a vital role in ensuring that the information gathered during the monitoring phase translates into actionable changes. After data is collected and analyzed, it is crucial that findings are communicated back to the employees involved in the safety program. This communication can take many forms, including safety meetings, training sessions, and information bulletins. By creating a culture where employees feel informed about safety outcomes and involved in the decision-making process, organizations can reinforce positive behaviors and motivate staff to engage in safer practices actively. Additionally, feedback allows employees to share their observations and suggestions, which further enriches the data pool and enhances overall safety performance.

Integrating these continuous improvement mechanisms within BBS programs fosters a proactive safety culture. As employees see their input leading to improvements and changes based on analytics, their engagement and commitment to safety are likely to increase. Moreover, organizations can adopt cyclic models, such as Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA), where they systematically plan for improvements, implement them, monitor outcomes, and adjust strategies accordingly. This not only enhances the safety environment but also contributes to a sustainable program that aligns with an organization’s overall mission and goals. In conclusion, the synergy between continuous improvement and feedback loops underpinned by data analytics is essential for nurturing an effective Behavior-Based Safety program that is responsive and adaptive to the evolving safety landscape.

![SafetyCulture](/shared/logo/safetyculture.svg)

![Google Play](/shared/app/en/google-play.svg)

# A Short Guide to Data Collection Techniques

Learn more about data collection methods and techniques. Choose which would work best for you.

## What are Data Collection Techniques?

Data collection techniques refer to methods used to collect and analyze different forms of data. Standard data collection techniques include going through documents related to a topic, conducting interviews with people who know about the subject, and listing observations that can help solidify the information gathered.

## Kinds of Data

Generally, [data collection](https://safetyculture.com/topics/data-collection/) can yield two kinds of data: [qualitative and quantitative](https://libguides.macalester.edu/c.php?g=527786&p=3608639).

Both quantitative and qualitative data have similar data collection techniques, as they often work together to help create a more in-depth analysis of the data.

## Benefits Using Different Data Collection Methods

Data collection techniques can offer a number of benefits to businesses and organizations of all sizes, from small startups to Fortune 500 companies.

## Applications

Data collection techniques are becoming increasingly popular in various business areas. For example, healthcare institutions can utilize these techniques to collect data on patient histories, patient records, and other critical information. Similarly, they can also be used in logistics and transportation to collect data on shipments and routes for improved business operations.

Overall, data collection methods offer several benefits to businesses of all types. By deploying the right technique, they can maximize the value and insights from their data to get more from their investments in technology.

## 7 Data Collection Techniques

![7 Data Collection Techniques](/_next/image/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwp-website.safetyculture.com%2Fwp-content%2Fuploads%2Fsites%2F3%2F2023%2F12%2F7-Data-Collection-Techniques.png&w=1920&q=75)

Data Collection Techniques

There are many ways to collect data depending on the data needed and the tasks at hand. Here are seven of the most common data collection techniques in business analytics as said by [Harvard Business School](https://online.hbs.edu/blog/post/data-collection-methods).

### 1. Observations

The easiest and most direct data collection technique is observation. The [most common form of observation](https://opentext.wsu.edu/carriecuttler/chapter/observational-research/) in the context of data collection involves simply watching the behaviors or actions of a subject in a specific setting to understand them and record what was observed. In today’s online world, an example of the act of observation can include watching people interact with products, websites, and services in real-time.

### 2. Interviews and Focus Groups

Another direct data collection technique is conducting interviews and focus groups. A focus group is a kind of interview-like conversation that happens in a group of [six to twelve people](https://www.cdc.gov/healthyyouth/evaluation/pdf/brief13.pdf) who share a common interest, characteristic, or need. The group will have a facilitator present them questions to discuss together. The aim of having an interview and a focus group discussion is to gain more information and depth on different topics, perceptions, beliefs, and attitudes in an environment where they all come together.

This data collection technique can become more streamlined with the help of a [focus group template](https://safetyculture.com/checklists/focus-groups/).

### 3. Transactional Tracking

Transactional tracking is a data collection technique that relies on one’s purchases to derive data from. With each purchase made by a customer, researchers and sellers can access data from their websites, a third-party service provider, or from their e-commerce in-store point of sale system. From there, they can track different forms and amounts of data, allowing them to create better marketing plans and products, and target their ideal customers. Tracking customers’ transactions is also a good way to know and understand them better, as their purchases can say a lot about them.

### 4. Social Media Monitoring

Social media monitoring is a data collection technique similar to transactional tracking. However, instead of a customer’s transaction history, this kind of technique focuses on tracking one’s social media history and footprint. Many platforms and businesses use this to track one’s engagement with different posts online to better understand what products and services they would be interested in, as well as what they consider important to them. From this, businesses can better target their customers with more fitting advertisements and more relevant products.

### 5. Online Tracking

Another data collection technique similar to transaction tracking and social media monitoring is online tracking. Unlike the other internet-based data collection techniques, online tracking is more general and can be done on sites other than e-commerce or social media ones through the use of [cookies](https://www.cookiepro.com/knowledge/tracking-cookie/). Through online tracking, data can be collected from marketing campaigns run through search engine results, webpage advertisements, email campaigns, and other places where one’s brand can be showcased. As long as it is online, it can be tracked.

To successfully carry out online tracking as a data collection technique, one would need to have specific software to help them analyze the numbers and behaviors of their customers online. Some things this kind of software dedicated to online tracking can tell are the number of times a certain link is clicked, the device used by customers, ads clicked, location, and more.

### 6. Surveys

Surveys are one of the most well-known methods of data collection. They are done with questionnaires and can be conducted physically and digitally to collect both quantitative and qualitative data. These questionnaires are often inexpensive to create and answer, thus making surveys a very accessible option for both researchers and their correspondents.

Commonly, surveys are used to collect responses about an event or item. The responses collected can then be used as a basis for [product improvement](https://safetyculture.com/topics/product-improvement/), [decision-making matrices](https://safetyculture.com/checklists/decision-matrix-template/), or further studies.

### 7. Forms

Similar to surveys, forms try to gather data through a set of questions. However, unlike surveys, forms can be more general. Often, forms are used to gather qualitative data from a subject or a group of subjects, particularly their demographic data or contact details. Forms are also used to get in touch with one’s potential clients and get to know them better.

## How to Make Effective Use of Data Collection Methods

When it comes to using data collection techniques effectively, there are 5 key steps to keep in mind:

### 1. Identify the Audience and Type of Data to be Collected.

First and foremost, it is important to define the target audience and determine the specific data that needs to be collected. This will allow the data collection technique to be as applicable as possible to provide the most accurate results.

### 2. Map the Data Collection Process.

Once the target audience and data have been determined, plan out the data collection process. When setting up a data collection technique, it is essential to ensure that the method is comprehensive and can account for any data that may be missing.

Additionally, a data collection technique should consider how the data will be collected, and how the data can be interpreted and used effectively.

### 3. Analyze the Results.

When the data has been collected and all steps have been taken to ensure accuracy, it is important to analyze the results.

### 4. Observe the Relationship Between Data Points.

Once the analysis is complete, it is possible to see how the data is related to each other and determine the answers to any questions posed by the data. By understanding the relationships between different data points and the trends that they indicate, effective decisions can be made.

### 5. Communicate Your Findings.

Lastly, it is essential to effectively communicate the data collection technique findings. By clearly communicating the results, the gathered insights can be used to solve real-world problems, make informed decisions about a business or organization, or even just learn and better understand how things work.

--- SOURCE 4: Advancing safety analytics: A diagnostic framework for assessing ... ---
URL: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/

SUMMARY:
The proposed framework suggests that safety analytics readiness depends on (a) the quality of the data available, (b) organizational norms around data collection, scaling, and nomenclature, (c) foundational infrastructure, including technological platforms and skills required for data collection, storage, and analysis of health and safety metrics, and (d) measurement culture, or the emergent social patterns between employees, data acquisition, and analytic processes. Overall, analytics within OSH will be more successful (e.g., value) in identifying trends that lead to risk mitigation when there is more (e.g., volume) high quality (e.g., veracity) data of varied type (e.g., variety of data type) over time, considering both archival and continuously updated current data (e.g., velocity), originating from safety as well as other organizational units (e.g., variety of source; quality control, production, human resources).

Advancing safety analytics: A diagnostic framework for assessing system readiness within occupational safety and health
=======================================================================================================================

[Maira E Ezerins](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=%22Ezerins%20ME%22%5BAuthor%5D)

The publisher's version of this article is available at [Saf Sci](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ssci.2021.105569)

Abstract
--------

Big data and analytics have shown promise in predicting safety incidents and identifying preventative measures directed towards specific risk variables. However, the safety industry is lagging in big data utilization due to various obstacles, which may include lack of data readiness (e.g., disparate databases, missing data, low validity) and personnel competencies. This paper provides a primer on the application of big data to safety. We then describe a safety analytics readiness assessment framework that highlights system requirements and the challenges that safety professionals may encounter in meeting these requirements. The proposed framework suggests that safety analytics readiness depends on (a) the quality of the data available, (b) organizational norms around data collection, scaling, and nomenclature, (c) foundational infrastructure, including technological platforms and skills required for data collection, storage, and analysis of health and safety metrics, and (d) measurement culture, or the emergent social patterns between employees, data acquisition, and analytic processes. A safety-analytics readiness assessment can assist organizations with understanding current capabilities so measurement systems can be matured to accommodate more advanced analytics for the ultimate purpose of improving decisions that mitigate injury and incidents.

**Keywords:** Safety analytics, Data analytics, Readiness assessment, Occupational health

1. Introduction
---------------

Safety incidents have a major impact on the workforce and organizations ([Bureau of Labor Statistics [BLS], 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/#R12)). There are approximately 2.8 million injuries and illnesses within the private work sector in the United States annually ([BLS, 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/#R12), [2019](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/#R13), [2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/#R14)). Costs associated with these work injuries go beyond workers’ compensation. The [National Safety Council (2019)](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/#R60) estimates that work injuries cost companies $161.5 billion annually, from expenses such as wage and productivity losses ($50.7 billion); medical expenses ($34.3 billion); and administrative expenses ($52.0 billion).

Initiatives and regulations introduced by agencies in the United States, such as the Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA), have done much to reduce injuries and illnesses ([OSHA, 2012](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/#R64)). Organizations are required to notify OSHA when injuries and fatalities occur in the workplace and store large amounts of data that can be presented as proof of compliance with regulations when necessary ([OSHA, 2001](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/#R63)). As such, many industrial organizations have large amounts of safety data available that could be analyzed statistically.

Further, current analytics research within occupational safety has demonstrated the predictive capabilities of (a) demographic information, such as age, gender, and worker experience ([Chi et al., 2014](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/#R18); [Stewart, 2013](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/#R89)), (b) job-related information such as industry, equipment, job risk, and training ([Lingard et al., 2017](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/#R53)), and (c) behavioral information, such as the use of personal protective equipment, hazard identification, and housekeeping ([Mistikoglu et al., 2015](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/#R57)) in predicting adverse safety outcomes. Some of these models are quite sophisticated and have a high degree of accuracy. For example, Carnegie Mellon University ([Predictive Solutions, 2012](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/#R74)) incorporated 112 million safety observations and safety incident data from over 15,000 work sites to predict incidents with accuracy rates as high as 80–97 percent and _R_ _2_ as high as 0.75 between actual and predicted incidents.

Using predictive models such as these, organizations can direct scarce resources to the locations and work teams that are at highest risk of safety incidents ([Schultz, 2012](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/#R85)). For example, Deloitte analyzed five years of data on injuries, demographics, production, operations, and weather from Goldcorp, a gold-mining company ([Stewart, 2013](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/#R89)). Statistically significant relationships were found between injury rates and compensation, age, job roles and other operational factors. Goldcorp used these insights to increase managerial training, write new policies, and focus supervisor support on employees with higher risk profiles.

Despite the demonstrated success of safety analytics, these applications remain the exception and not the rule. Organizations have made limited progress in developing analytics for safety ([Tan et al., 2016](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/#R90)), and many safety professionals lack an understanding of what is possible with existing data pools ([Ferguson, 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/#R28)). In this paper, we provide a thorough primer on data attributes, analytic sophistication, and the application of data strategies to Occupational Safety and Health (OSH). We then highlight system requirements for advanced analytics and describe safety-specific challenges. In doing so, we develop a framework for assessing current safety analytics capabilities, maximizing analytic value, and making prioritization decisions for investment in system improvements.

1.1. Features of big data
-------------------------

Safety professionals have more data than ever at their fingertips ([Canitz, 2019](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/#R16)). In addition to artifacts of employee reporting, there is a flood of data being created by new safety devices that provide detailed information on people, machines, and the environment ([Ferguson, 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/#R28)). These increasingly large datasets approach the size of big data, defined as a lot of different types of data handled in new ways, or large datasets that tax system capacity ([Dastjerdi et al., 2016](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/#R22)). Big data is a term used ubiquitously within the analytics zeitgeist, yet it is distinguishable from other data forms and statistics through five defining features: volume, velocity, variety, value, and veracity ([Ramadan, 2017](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/#R77)). While big data often refers to data that is unmanageable by traditional architecture ([Goel et al., 2017](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/#R35)), these five features (see [Fig. 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/#F1)) can be useful in identifying the characteristics of any size data that contribute to sophisticated analytical output.

### Fig. 1.

[![Image 9: Fig. 1.](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/de14/10191184/4bf1f26375b8/nihms-1858494-f0001.jpg)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=10191184_nihms-1858494-f0001.jpg)

[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/figure/F1/)

Five Characteristics of Big Data.

Overall, analytics within OSH will be more successful (e.g., value) in identifying trends that lead to risk mitigation when there is more (e.g., volume) high quality (e.g., veracity) data of varied type (e.g., variety of data type) over time, considering both archival and continuously updated current data (e.g., velocity), originating from safety as well as other organizational units (e.g., variety of source; quality control, production, human resources).

Volume is defined as the quantity and size of data that are generated and stored. Increasingly, OSH is collecting data in volumes that are difficult or impossible for traditional databases and statistical methods to handle. Text analysis in R, for example, can only analyze a certain number of observation comments. For less advanced analytics, such as identifying trends by frequency count, volume is typically less of a concern. However, as OSH increases exponentially in data volume, constraints to conducting analytics may include man hour costs for personnel and technical expertise necessary to collect, aggregate, store, and analyze the high-volume data. Thus, as volume increases, OSH may need to invest in a dedicated position or allot significant time for incumbent staff to manage the safety data.

Velocity is defined as the speed at which data are generated and processed. In the past, high velocity was less of a challenge, as incident reports happened relatively infrequently, and other data collection mechanisms required manual entry. There are new technologies within safety (e.g., wearable ergonomic belts), however, that provide constantly streaming real-time information via sensors. Though high velocity data collection is not necessary for all analytics, data that are collected more frequently will allow for more precise temporal output of predictions, as continuously updating information makes real-time predictions possible. High velocity can lead to high volume, however, and OSH professionals will face challenges in maintaining pace in decision-making with the continuously updated information. In addition, practitioners will need to make decisions about timing of analyses (i.e., frequency of insight dissemination to managerial personnel) and retention periods.

Variety is defined as the heterogeneity of data type and nature, including the different sources of information across processes and functions, forms of data (e.g., electronic monitoring, fillable forms, surveys), and types of database structures utilized within the organization. This wide variety of data can make aggregation and analysis challenging. For example, data may be structured (e.g., text observations in a relational or tidy format), semi-structured (e.g., csv files) or unstructured (e.g., images and video). OSH faces obstacles in gaining or utilizing the technologies and infrastructure that allow for this data to be stored, aggregated, and analyzed despite these different formats.

The final features of big data—veracity and value—are essential characteristics of all data, not just big data ([Ramadan, 2017](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/#R77)). Veracity is defined by three dimensions of information quality: data objectivity, truthfulness, and credibility ([Lukoianova & Rubin, 2014](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/#R56)). The validity and reliability of data are rarely formally assessed yet analytical output is only as accurate or as trustworthy as data inputs; a concept succinctly summarized by the popular adage “garbage in, garbage out.” Veracity impacts data quality and analytic value, and big data is often plagued by biases, ambiguity, or error ([Lukoianova & Rubin, 2014](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/#R56)). Safety data relies heavily on employee and front-line supervisor reporting where rater errors in reporting can occur due to concerns over negative outcomes on their jobs such as discipline for rule violations, extra personal attention by management, or added cumbersome safety rules and equipment. These workplace variables may cause a degree of “pencil whipping” ([Ludwig, 2014](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/#R54)) whereby information entered into safety reports are underreported or not factual ([Probst & Graso, 2013](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/#R75)). In other instances, employees may not have the technical expertise to correctly categorize safety events and/or adequately describe the event. These and other rater errors can impact the validity and reliability of the data. Luckily, big data is typically of such volume that trends are often still useful. There are also methods of managing data veracity, such as identifying suspicious outliers, automated detection and sorting of quality using machine learning, or crowdsourcing techniques (see [Assiri, 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/#R3), [Table 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/#T1)).

### Table 1.

Variable List consisting of the 26 important variables that may impact safety outcomes, along with their definitions.

| Variable | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| * * * |
| **Safety Metrics** |  |
| Safety Metrics Hazard ID | The metrics surrounding the reports made by employees of hazardous environments that could lead to safety incidents. |
| Audits/Inspections | These metrics track the frequency of audits and inspections of workplaces and workgroups to measure if the work is being done safely and if the environment is safe. |
| First Aid/Minor Injury Reporting | These metrics track minor injuries, such as small cuts, trips, or falls that may need first aid. |
| Near Miss/Close Call Reporting | This metric measures the number of instances where employees report that a safety incident did not occur, but almost did. |
| Environmental | Environmental metrics track adverse conditions such as weather, heat, wind, storms, etc. |
| Behavioral Observations | Metrics collected on behavioral observations may include video or checklists, either paper and pencil forms or electronic. |
| Fatigue | Measures tracking the point at which employees are likely to have a safety incident due to strain and fatigue caused by the nature of their work/workload. |
| Safety Participation | Measures of safety participation track indirect and voluntary behaviors that contribute to a safe work environment, including participating in shift discussions, safety meetings, close call/near miss reporting, or investigating incidents. |
| Safety Culture | Safety culture measures the extent to which employees and managers think about, talk about, and are committed to having a safe workplace. |
| **Production** |  |
| Volume Trends | The quantity of product output at a given time. |
| Scheduled Events | These metrics relate to changes to the production that managers may make, such as switching a machine from one product to another. |
| Staffing Loads | These metrics relate to accounting for people working at a given time, and how much those employees are working. |
| Calendar Events | These are events that may center around a date on a calendar, like a holiday or vacation. |
| Quality | A measure of excellence or a state of being free from defects, deficiencies, and significant variations. These metrics may also measure the opposite, such as errors or reworks. |
| Cost/Budget | These metrics would measure estimated costs, revenues, and resources over a specified period. |
| **Maintenance** |  |
| Failures (Equipment) | These metrics track what machines have failed, how often, or why. |
| Action Item Backlog | These metrics measure unfinished tasks that need to be completed. |
| Preventative Maintenance | Data centered around equipment and facilities by tracking systematic inspection, detection, and correction of incipient failures either before they occur or before they develop into major defects. |
| **Procedures** |  |
| Change Management | These metrics could track what occurs after a change in procedure, product, or could track leadership changes over time. These organizational metrics may cross-functions and help to provide a strategic lens. |
| **Human Resources** |  |
| Safety Knowledge | Knowledge of how to perform tasks safely; affected by T&D practices. |
| Job Attitudes | Job satisfaction, organizational commitment, etc. |
| Safety motivation | Willingness to exert effort to enact safety behaviors. |
| Turnover | A measurement of the number of employees who leave an organization during a specified time period. |
| Absenteeism | The measure of the number of employees that are absent from their scheduled shift. |
| Employee Characteristics | Personality characteristics (e.g., Big 5, locus of control, risk taking propensity, demographics of the workforce that is being measured (e.g., tenure, sex, age, education). |
| Culture | The measure of the cultural norms of the workforce, including what they prioritize, talk about, and behave. |

[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/table/T1/)

The last feature of optimal data is also related to quality, but for outcomes. Value is defined as the measurable impact of data-driven insights. Value can thus refer to both inputs and outputs; data itself (input) has a value in how it can contribute towards data-driven insights, and those insights (output) have value when actions derived from data have a measurable impact (e.g., reliably predict risk profiles and mitigate injury). Each piece of information is more or less important to an analysis in terms of its relevancy to the question of interest, as well as how much value the information adds to the output statistically (e.g., increases in _R_ _2_ or explained variance). For example, within OSH, the data collected from smart wearable ergonomic belts are important in determining risk profiles for back injuries, and likely make up the bulk of the necessary information to determine risk factors, along with behaviors and equipment associated with the task. In contrast, hazard identification data would contribute to a risk profile for the same job but add less value to the prediction of back injuries. The “value” of analytics is thus created when the right objective and credible data (e.g., the sensory information from smart ergonomic belts) drives action (e.g., new standard operating procedures or targeted observations) that has measurable impact (e.g., fewer back injuries).

While these five features of big data are typically used to describe the challenges of data management on a huge scale, we argue that these characteristics can also be used to determine the level of readiness of smaller OSH datasets to successfully engage in advanced analytics. In addition, there exist common obstacles (e.g., difficulty in aggregating data of different forms) and techniques for mitigating these challenges (e.g., ‘data lake’ management systems for unstructured data). This comparison between big data characteristics and analytics within OSH may also aid organizations in determining the level of statistical methods or analytics that organizations can aspire to, given the constraints of their data characteristics.

### 1.2. Levels of analytics

There are five levels of analytics that offer increasing degrees of sophistication in terms of their ability to drive improved decision-making ([Fred & Kinange, 2015](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/#R32)): (a) descriptive analytics, (b) diagnostic analytics, (c) predictive analytics, and (d) prescriptive analytics, and (e) cognitive analytics ([Canitz, 2019](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/#R16); [Lepenioti et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/#R50)). Descriptive analytics answer questions about what has happened in the past. Data are analyzed for characteristics and relationships through data visualizations and statistics such as sums, means, and averages ([Huang et al., 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/#R42)). In OSH, descriptive analytics may look at the number of behavioral observations or equipment inspections in a month and distribution curves across different departments in the company. Dashboards and reports are also a form of descriptive analytic technique.

Diagnostic analytics provide clues about the reason for such past occurrences. These types of analytics (e.g., correlational analysis) describe relationships between variables to provide context. Diagnostic analytics use historical and past safety performance to identify reasons for the success or failure of initiatives—or explanations for specific outcomes—by investigating relationships, outliers, and sequences ([Huang et al., 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/#R42)). An example of a diagnostic analytic technique in OSH would include a root cause analysis ([Canitz, 2019](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/#R16)). Safety data analytics at this level has focused on correlates to workplace injury such as external pressures, internal social context, and organization characteristics including job demands (e.g., environmental conditions, scheduling and workload, physical job demands, and the overall complexity of work; [Barling et al., 2002](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/#R8)), leadership (e.g., relationship with the manager, leadership style, trust, and accountability; [Fogarty, 2004](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/#R29)), and organizational commitment to safety ([Fogarty, 2004](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/#R29)).

Predictive analytics (e.g., regression analysis) attempt to prognosticate future outcomes to answer questions about which incidents are likely to happen and why. In addition to historical data, predictive analysis incorporates current information in an attempt to predict the likelihood of an event ([Huang et al., 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/#R42)). Predictive analytics in OSH can include both short-term and longer-term predictions. For example, [Lingard et al. (2017)](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/#R53) used injury rate and other data from a large construction company’s safety program (e.g., toolbox talks, prestart meetings, safety observations, hazards reported, etc.) to identify a lagging predictive pattern between indicators. Their findings suggested that injuries were often followed by an increase in preventative measures (e. g., toolbox talks) which subsequently decreased until the occurrence of the next injury. In another example, a construction contracting company in Singapore used five different types of machine learning models on project- and safety-related variables to predict conditions under which no accidents would be likely to occur, conditions which may cause minor accidents, and conditions which may cause major accidents. The most effective of these, a random forest regression, a type of decision tree, predicted these conditions with 78% accuracy ([Poh et al., 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/#R72)).

Prescriptive analytics take results from predictive analytics and utilize real-time data streams to provide more accurate guidance for decision-making ([Mousanif et al., 2014](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/#R59)). Safety data, mathematical formulae, safety rules, and machine learning are used in continuously updated models to suggest the most advantageous real-time decision options based on the identification of future opportunities or risks ([Huang et al., 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/#R42)). For example, [Ayhan et al. (2018)](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10191184/#R4) built a Viterbi algorithm variant (i.e., a dynamic programming algorithm that finds the most likely of events within a sequence) to reduce the chances of air traffic and collisions by predicting when an airplane trajectory may infringe on the protected zone of another aircraft. The prescriptive model detects and resolves these potential conflicts before aircraft even depart, resulting in safer operations, higher efficiency, higher capacity, and redu

enriched_points.expanded_content: Surveys collect quantitative and qualitative data from employees regarding safety perceptions and practices, enabling organizations to gauge awareness and areas needing improvement.
Interviews provide in-depth insights into employee experiences and attitudes towards safety, allowing for nuanced understanding of safety culture and individual concerns.
Case studies offer detailed examinations of specific incidents or practices, helping organizations learn from real-world scenarios and identify effective safety interventions.
Literature reviews synthesize existing research on safety analytics, providing a foundational understanding of methodologies and best practices to inform organizational strategies.
